pages.title: Geometry & Materials
pages.body:
# Geometry & Materials

Artlab wraps Three.js geometry into simple factory functions. Five primitives are visible in the preview — a sphere, box, torus, cylinder, and cone.

Each shape is created from a geometry + material pair and added to the scene with ctx.add().

Use → to explore each concept in detail, ↑ to go back to a parent topic, and ← to go to the previous page.

pages.title: Primitive Shapes
pages.body:
# Primitive Shapes

Six common primitives are available as factory functions on ctx:

```
sphere(radius)             → SphereGeometry
box(width, height, depth)  → BoxGeometry
torus(R, r)                → TorusGeometry
cylinder(radius, height)   → CylinderGeometry
cone(radius, height)       → ConeGeometry
plane(width, height)       → PlaneGeometry
```

Each returns a THREE.BufferGeometry you pass to mesh().

Under the hood these are standard Three.js geometries — you can also construct them directly via ctx.THREE if you need custom segment counts.

pages.title: Materials
pages.body:
# Materials

Artlab uses MeshStandardMaterial by default — a physically-based material that responds to lights realistically.

Key parameters:

```
mesh(geo, {
  color:     0x4466ff,  // base albedo hex
  roughness: 0.35,      // 0 = mirror, 1 = matte
  metalness: 0.15,      // 0 = plastic, 1 = metal
  emissive:  0x112244,  // self-illumination color
})
```

## Roughness vs Metalness

- Rough metal → brushed aluminium look
- Smooth metal → chrome mirror
- Rough non-metal → chalk, stone, matte paint
- Smooth non-metal → polished plastic, lacquer
